Option<bool>,\n    section7_raw: Option<Vec<u8>>,\n}\n\nimpl LazyFieldBuilder {\n    fn build(self) -> Option<LazyField> {\n        let center = self.center?;\n        let subcenter = self.subcenter?;\n        let ref_time = self.ref_time?;\n        let parameter = self.parameter?;\n        let mut forecast = self.forecast?;\n        forecast.reference_time = ref_time;\n        let level = self.level?;\n        let ensemble = self.ensemble.unwrap_or(None);\n        let grid = self.grid?;\n        let gdt_template = self.gdt_template?;\n        let pdt_template = self.pdt_template?;\n        let packing = self.packing?;\n        let drt_template = self.drt_template?;\n        let has_bitmap = self.has_bitmap.unwrap_or(false);\n        let section7_raw = self.section7_raw.unwrap_or_default();\n        let complex_extra = self.complex_extra;\n        Some(LazyField {\n            center, subcenter, parameter, forecast, level, ensemble,\n            grid, packing, gdt_template, pdt_template, drt_template,\n            has_bitmap, section7_raw, complex_extra,\n        })\n    }\n}\n\nfn decode_lazy_message(msg: &[u8], out: &mut Vec<LazyField>) -> Result<usize> {\n    let mut buf = Buf::new(msg);\n    let magic = [buf.read_u8()?, buf.read_u8()?, buf.read_u8()?, buf.read_u8()?];\n    if &magic != b\"GRIB\" {\n        return Err(Error::BadMagic(magic));\n    }\n    buf.skip(2)?;\n    let discipline = buf.read_u8()?;\n    let edition = buf.read_u8()?;\n    if edition != 2 {\n        return Err(Error::UnknownEdition(edition));\n    }\n    let total_len = buf.read_u64be()? as usize;\n    if msg.len() < total_len {\n        return Err(Error::TooShort { needed: total_len, got: msg.len() });\n    }\n\n    let mut builder = LazyFieldBuilder::default();\n    let body_end = total_len - 4;\n\n    while buf.pos < body_end {\n        let sec_start = buf.pos;\n        let sec_len = buf.read_u32be()? as usize;\n        let sec_num = buf.read_u8()?;\n        if sec_len < 5 {\n            return Err(Error::TooShort { needed: 5, got: sec_len });\n        }\n        let body_start = buf.pos;\n        let body_len = sec_len - 5;\n        let sec_body = &msg[body_start..body_start + body_len];\n\n        match sec_num {\n            1 => {\n                let (center, subcenter, ref_time) = parse_section1(sec_body)?;\n                builder.center = Some(center);\n                builder.subcenter = Some(subcenter);\n                builder.ref_time = Some(ref_time);\n            }\n            2 => {}\n            3 => {\n                let (gdt, grid) = parse_section3(sec_body)?;\n                builder.gdt_template = Some(gdt);\n                builder.grid = Some(grid);\n            }\n            4 => {\n                let (pdt, param, fore, lvl, ens) = parse_section4(sec_body, discipline)?;\n                builder.pdt_template = Some(pdt);\n                builder.parameter = Some(param);\n                builder.forecast = Some(fore);\n                builder.level = Some(lvl);\n                builder.ensemble = Some(ens);\n            }\n            5 => {\n                // Store the full packing info including complex extras for DRT=2/3.\n                let (drt, packing, extra, _n_packed) = parse_section5(sec_body)?;\n                builder.drt_template = Some(drt);\n                builder.packing = Some(packing);\n                builder.complex_extra = extra;\n            }\n            6 => {\n                let (has_bitmap, _bitmap) = parse_section6(sec_body)?;\n                builder.has_bitmap = Some(has_bitmap);\n            }\n            7 => {\n                // Store raw bytes for DRT=0, DRT=2, and DRT=3 without a bitmap.\n                // DRT=0: single-point random access via decode_point_drt0.\n                // DRT=2/3: full-grid decode via decode_all_drt3 (spatial differencing\n                //          prevents true random access, so the full grid must be decoded).\n                let drt = builder.drt_template.unwrap_or(u16::MAX);\n                let no_bitmap = !builder.has_bitmap.unwrap_or(false);\n                let raw = if (drt == 0 || drt == 2 || drt == 3) && no_bitmap {\n                    sec_body.to_vec()\n                } else {\n                    Vec::new()\n                };\n                builder.section7_raw = Some(raw);\n\n                let next_builder = LazyFieldBuilder {\n                    center: builder.center,\n                    subcenter: builder.subcenter,\n                    ref_time: builder.ref_time,\n                    ..Default::default()\n                };\n                if let Some(field) = builder.build() {\n                    out.push(field);\n                }\n                builder = next_builder;\n            }\n            _ => {}\n        }\n        buf.pos = sec_start + sec_len;\n    }\n\n    let end = &msg[body_end..body_end + 4];\n    if end != b\"7777\" {\n        return Err(Error::TooShort { needed: 4, got: 0 });\n    }\n    Ok(total_len)\n}\n\n// ── Tests ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────\n\n#[cfg(test)]\nmod tests {\n    use super::*;\n\n    #[test]\n    fn unpack_8bit_values() {\n        let data: Vec<u8> = (0u8..25).collect();\n        let values = unpack_n_bits(&data, 25, 8);\n        assert_eq!(values.len(), 25);\n        for (i, &v) in values.iter().enumerate() {\n            assert_eq!(v, i as u64, \"mismatch at index {}\", i);\n        }\n    }\n\n    #[test]\n    fn unpack_4bit_values() {\n        // 4-bit values: pack two per byte.\n        // data[0] = 0x01 → values 0, 1\n        // data[1] = 0x23 → values 2, 3\n        let data = [0x01u8, 0x23];\n        let values = unpack_n_bits(&data, 4, 4);\n        assert_eq!(values, vec![0, 1, 2, 3]);\n    }\n\n    #[test]\n    fn unpack_12bit_values() {\n        // [0, 1, 2] packed as 12-bit MSB-first values (36 bits → 5 bytes):\n        // val0=0x000: 0000 0000 0000\n        // val1=0x001: 0000 0000 0001\n        // val2=0x002: 0000 0000 0010\n        // byte0=0x00 byte1=0x00 byte2=0x01 byte3=0x00 byte4=0x20(+4 padding)\n        let data = [0x00u8, 0x00, 0x01, 0x00, 0x20];\n        let values = unpack_n_bits(&data, 3, 12);\n        assert_eq!(values, vec![0, 1, 2]);\n    }\n\n    #[test]\n    fn constant_field_zero_bits() {\n        let values = unpack_n_bits(&[], 5, 0);\n        assert_eq!(values, vec![0; 5]);\n    }\n\n    #[test]\n    fn decode_section7_simple_packing() {\n        let packing = PackingInfo {\n            reference_value: 270.0,\n            binary_scale_factor: 0,\n            decimal_scale_factor: 0,\n            bits_per_value: 8,\n            original_field_type: 0,\n        };\n        let data: Vec<u8> = (0u8..25).collect();\n        // DRT=0: no complex extra\n        let values = decode_section7(&data, &packing, 0, None, 25).unwrap();\n        match values {\n            GridValues::Dense(v) => {\n                assert_eq!(v.len(), 25);\n                for (i, &val) in v.iter().enumerate() {\n                    assert!((val - (270.0 + i as f64)).abs() < 1e-9, \"index {i}: {val}\");\n                }\n            }\n            _ => panic!(\"expected Dense\"),\n        }\n    }\n\n    #[test]\n    fn decode_point_drt0_matches_full_decode() {\n        // 8-bit packing: value[i] = 270 + i\n        let packing = PackingInfo {\n            reference_value: 270.0,\n            binary_scale_factor: 0,\n            decimal_scale_factor: 0,\n            bits_per_value: 8,\n            original_field_type: 0,\n        };\n        let data: Vec<u8> = (0u8..25).collect();\n        let full = decode_section7(&data, &packing, 0, None, 25).unwrap();\n        let GridValues::Dense(full_vals) = full else { panic!() };\n\n        for (idx, &full_val) in full_vals.iter().enumerate().take(25) {\n            let lazy_val = decode_point_drt0(&data, &packing, idx).expect(\"idx in range\");\n            assert!(\n                (lazy_val - full_val).abs() < 1e-9,\n                \"idx={idx}: lazy={lazy_val} full={full_val}\",\n            );\n        }\n        assert!(decode_point_drt0(&data, &packing, 25).is_none()); // out of range\n    }\n\n    #[test]\n    fn decode_point_drt0_constant_field() {\n        // 0-bit packing: all values equal R / 10^D\n        let packing = PackingInfo {\n            reference_value: 300.0,\n            binary_scale_factor: 0,\n            decimal_scale_factor: 0,\n            bits_per_value: 0,\n            original_field_type: 0,\n        };\n        // Any idx returns 300.0 (constant field, no body bytes needed)\n        assert_eq!(decode_point_drt0(&[], &packing, 0), Some(300.0));\n        assert_eq!(decode_point_drt0(&[], &packing, 999), Some(300.0));\n    }\n\n    #[test]\n    fn decode_point_drt3_matches_full_decode() {\n        // Verify decode_point_drt3 returns the same value as decode_all_drt3[idx]\n        // for the DRT=3 GFS fixture (gfs_tmp2m_1deg_anl).\n        let corpus_root = {\n            let manifest_dir = env!(\"CARGO_MANIFEST_DIR\");\n            std::path::Path::new(manifest_dir)\n                .join(\"../..\")\n                .join(\"tests/corpus\")\n        };\n        let fixture = corpus_root.join(\"small/gfs_tmp2m_1deg_anl.grib2\");\n        let bytes = match std::fs::read(&fixture) {\n            Ok(b) => b,\n            Err(_) => return, // skip if fixture not present\n        };\n        let lazy_fields = decode_bytes_lazy(&bytes).expect(\"lazy decode\");\n        for lf in &lazy_fields {\n            if lf.drt_template != 3 || lf.section7_raw.is_empty() {\n                continue;\n            }\n            let extra = lf.complex_extra.as_ref().expect(\"DRT=3 must have complex_extra\");\n            let n_pts = lf.grid.num_data_points as usize;\n            let all_vals = decode_all_drt3(&lf.section7_raw, &lf.packing, extra, n_pts)\n                .expect(\"decode_all_drt3 ok\");\n            // Verify first 5, middle, last index.\n            let indices: Vec<usize> = (0..5.min(n_pts))\n                .chain([n_pts / 2, n_pts.saturating_sub(1)])\n                .collect();\n            for idx in indices {\n                let point_val = decode_point_drt3(\n                    &lf.section7_raw, &lf.packing, extra, n_pts, idx,\n                ).expect(\"in-range idx\");\n                assert!(\n                    (point_val - all_vals[idx]).abs() < 1e-9,\n                    \"idx={idx}: point={point_val} all={}\", all_vals[idx],\n                );\n            }\n            // Out-of-range must return None.\n            assert!(\n                decode_point_drt3(&lf.section7_raw, &lf.packing, extra, n_pts, n_pts).is_none(),\n                \"out-of-range idx must return None\",\n            );\n        }\n    }\n\n    /// Verify extract_group_windowed matches read_bits_at across many (skip, w) combos.\n    #[test]\n    fn extract_group_windowed_matches_read_bits_at() {\n        // Pseudo-random but deterministic 64-byte data buffer.\n        let data: Vec<u8> = (0u8..=255).cycle().take(64).collect();\n        let gref = 7i64;\n        let count = 10;\n\n        for skip in 0..8usize {\n            for w in 1usize..=20 {\n                // Reference: per-element read_bits_at\n                let mut expected = Vec::with_capacity(count);\n                for k in 0..count {\n                    let v = read_bits_at(&data, skip + k * w, w) as i64;\n                    expected.push(gref + v);\n                }\n\n                // Windowed extractor under test\n                let mut actual = Vec::with_capacity(count);\n                extract_group_windowed(&data, skip, w, count, gref, &mut actual);\n\n                assert_eq!(\n                    expected, actual,\n                    \"skip={skip} w={w}: windowed result differs from read_bits_at\",\n                );\n            }\n        }\n    }\n\n    #[test]\n    fn decode_bytes_lazy_matches_decode_bytes() {\n        // Verify lazy parse of the 5×5 synthetic fixture against full decode.\n        // We test the structure (grid, packing) and that decode_point_drt0\n        // produces values matching the full decode at every index.\n        let corpus_root = {\n            let manifest_dir = env!(\"CARGO_MANIFEST_DIR\");\n            std::path::Path::new(manifest_dir)\n                .join(\"../..\")\n                .join(\"tests/corpus\")\n        };\n        let fixture = corpus_root.join(\"small/gfs_anl_t2m_5x5.grib2\");\n        let bytes = match std::fs::read(&fixture) {\n            Ok(b) => b,\n            Err(_) => return, // skip if fixture not present (CI may not have corpus)\n        };\n\n        let full_fields = decode_bytes(&bytes).expect(\"full decode\");\n        let lazy_fields = decode_bytes_lazy(&bytes).expect(\"lazy decode\");\n\n        assert_eq!(full_fields.len(), lazy_fields.len());\n        for (ff, lf) in full_fields.iter().zip(lazy_fields.iter()) {\n            assert_eq!(ff.grid, lf.grid);\n            assert_eq!(ff.packing, lf.packing);\n            assert_eq!(ff.drt_template, lf.drt_template);\n            assert!(!lf.has_bitmap);\n            assert!(!lf.section7_raw.is_empty(), \"DRT=0 should have raw bytes\");\n\n            let GridValues::Dense(ref full_vals) = ff.values else { panic!(\"expected Dense\") };\n            for (idx, &full_val) in full_vals.iter().enumerate() {\n                let lazy_val = decode_point_drt0(&lf.section7_raw, &lf.packing, idx)\n                    .expect(\"idx in range\");\n                let tol = lf.packing.tolerance().max(1e-12);\n                assert!(\n                    (lazy_val - full_val).abs() <= tol,\n                    \"idx={idx}: lazy={lazy_val} full={full_val}\",\n                );\n            }\n        }\n    }\n}\n","numLines":2156,"startLine":1,"totalLines":2156}}}

{"type":"user","message":{"role":"user","content":[{"tool_use_id":"call_d6b926b971244c529e23f446","type":"tool_result","content":"1\t# gribtract\n2\t\n3\tA pure-Rust GRIB2 decoder — decodes NOAA/WMO GRIB2 messages into typed fields and gridded numeric data, verified field-by-field against eccodes/wgrib2.\n4\t\n5\tNo C dependencies for core GRIB2 decoding (DRT 5.0, 5.2, 5.3, 5.41). No shelling out to `wgrib2`. JPEG2000 (DRT 5.40) requires the optional `jpeg2000` feature, which pulls in `openjpeg-sys` (C FFI).\n6\t\n7\t## What is GRIB2?\n8\t\n9\tGRIB2 (GRIdded Binary, Edition 2) is the binary file format used by NOAA, ECMWF, and other national weather services to distribute model output — temperature, wind, precipitation, pressure fields on global and regional grids. It is the primary output format for GFS, HRRR, NBM, GEFS, NAM, and similar NWP (numerical weather prediction) products.\n10\t\n11\tDecoding GRIB2 requires handling: multi-section message framing, grid geometry definitions (lat/lon, Lambert conformal, polar stereographic, Gaussian), parameter tables indexed by discipline/category/number, five or more data packing algorithms (simple, complex, spatial-differencing, JPEG2000, PNG), and bitmaps for missing-value masking.\n12\t\n13\tThe standard tools (`wgrib2`, `eccodes`) are C programs and libraries. If you need to decode GRIB2 in a Rust project, your options have been to shell out to them, call their C APIs through FFI, or skip decoded values and work with raw bytes.\n14\t\n15\tgribtract is a native Rust alternative.\n16\t\n17\t## Features\n18\t\n19\t- **Pure Rust for core formats** — no C toolchain or FFI for DRT 5.0, 5.2, 5.3, 5.41 (simple, complex, complex+spatial differencing, PNG). JPEG2000 (DRT 5.40) requires the optional `jpeg2000` feature, which uses `openjpeg-sys` (C FFI)\n20\t- **Typed output** — decoded fields carry structured metadata: parameter ID, level, forecast time, grid geometry\n21\t- **Multiple packing formats** — DRT 5.0 (simple), 5.2 (complex), 5.3 (complex+spatial differencing), 5.40 (JPEG2000), 5.41 (PNG)\n22\t- **Multiple grid projections** — lat/lon (GDT 3.0), polar stereographic (3.20), Lambert conformal (3.30), Gaussian (3.40)\n23\t- **Lazy decode path** — parse headers and defer grid data; extract individual grid points in O(1) for DRT=0 fields without full decode\n24\t- **Timeseries extractor** — given a decoded GRIB2 message set, extract per-station forecasts for a list of lat/lon coordinates\n25\t- **Python bindings** — `gribtract-py` (PyO3) exposes the Rust decoder to Python\n26\t- **Oracle-gated correctness** — every release is verified against eccodes/wgrib2 over a corpus of real NOAA files\n27\t\n28\t## Crate Layout\n29\t\n30\t```\n31\tgribtract/\n32\t├── crates/\n33\t│   ├── gribtract-core     # Section parser, template decoders, unpacking. No I/O.\n34\t│   ├── gribtract          # High-level API: message iterator, field selection, timeseries\n35\t│   ├── gribtract-cli      # `gribtract decode|list|dump` command-line tool\n36\t│   ├── gribtract-py       # Python bindings via PyO3 (excluded from default build)\n37\t│   └── gribtract-testutil # Differential harness utilities: corpus, golden, diff\n38\t└── xtask                  # Corpus management, reference-decoder runner, bench dashboard\n39\t```\n40\t\n41\t`gribtract-core` is the zero-dependency parsing core. `gribtract` is the crate you depend on for library use. `gribtract-cli` provides a standalone binary.\n42\t\n43\t## Usage\n44\t\n45\tAdd to `Cargo.toml`:\n46\t\n47\t```toml\n48\t[dependencies]\n49\tgribtract = \"0.1\"\n50\t```\n51\t\n52\t### Decode all fields from a GRIB2 file\n53\t\n54\t```rust\n55\tuse gribtract::{decode, Field};\n56\t\n57\tlet bytes = std::fs::read(\"hrrr.t00z.wrfsfcf01.grib2\")?;\n58\tlet fields: Vec<Field> = gribtract::decode(&bytes)?;\n59\t\n60\tfor field in &fields {\n61\t    println!(\n62\t        \"param={}/{}/{}  level={}  forecast_offset={}h\",\n63\t        field.parameter.discipline,\n64\t        field.parameter.category,\n65\t        field.parameter.number,\n66\t        field.level.value1(),\n67\t        field.forecast_time.offset_seconds() / 3600,\n68\t    );\n69\t}\n70\t```\n71\t\n72\t### Extract a single grid point (DRT=0, O(1))\n73\t\n74\t```rust\n75\tuse gribtract::{decode_lazy, decode_point_drt0};\n76\t\n77\tlet fields = gribtract::decode_lazy(&bytes)?;\n78\tfor lf in &fields {\n79\t    if let Some(body) = &lf.section7_raw {\n80\t        // idx is the flat grid index for your target lat/lon\n81\t        let idx = lf.grid.nearest_index(40.64, -73.78)?;\n82\t        if let Some(value) = decode_point_drt0(body, &lf.packing, idx) {\n83\t            println!(\"value at JFK: {}\", value);\n84\t        }\n85\t    }\n86\t}\n87\t```\n88\t\n89\t### Extract a forecast timeseries for a list of stations\n90\t\n91\t```rust\n92\tuse gribtract::timeseries::{extract_timeseries, ParameterRecord, Station, TimeseriesRequest};\n93\t\n94\t// 2m temperature (discipline=0, category=0, number=0), height-above-ground level (type=103)\n95\tlet request = TimeseriesRequest {\n96\t    parameter: ParameterRecord { discipline: 0, category: 0, number: 0 },\n97\t    level_type1: 103,\n98\t    stations: vec![\n99\t        Station { id: \"JFK\".into(),  lat: 40.64,  lon: -73.78  },\n100\t        Station { id: \"ORD\".into(),  lat: 41.979, lon: -87.905 },\n101\t        Station { id: \"LAX\".into(),  lat: 33.943, lon: -118.408 },\n102\t    ],\n103\t};\n104\t\n105\tlet fields = gribtract::decode(&bytes)?;\n106\tlet ts = extract_timeseries(&fields, &request);\n107\t\n108\tfor (station, rows) in &ts.stations {\n109\t    println!(\"Station: {}\", station);\n110\t    for row in rows {\n111\t        println!(\"  forecast hour {}: {:?}\", row.forecast_hour, row.value);\n112\t    }\n113\t}\n114\t```\n115\t\n116\t### Decode a DRT=3 (complex+spatial differencing) grid — extract many stations\n117\t\n118\tDRT=3 requires a full sequential decode; random access is not possible. The recommended pattern is decode-once-extract-many:\n119\t\n120\t```rust\n121\tuse gribtract::{decode_lazy, decode_all_drt3};\n122\t\n123\tlet lazy_fields = gribtract::decode_lazy(&bytes)?;\n124\tfor lf in &lazy_fields {\n125\t    if let (Some(body), Some(extra)) = (&lf.section7_raw, &lf.complex_extra) {\n126\t        let n = lf.grid.num_data_points as usize;\n127\t        let grid: Vec<f64> = decode_all_drt3(body, &lf.packing, extra, n)?;\n128\t\n129\t        // Now extract any number of stations by index in O(1) each\n130\t        for (lat, lon) in &station_coords {\n131\t            let idx = lf.grid.nearest_index(*lat, *lon)?;\n132\t            println!(\"value: {}\", grid[idx]);\n133\t        }\n134\t    }\n135\t}\n136\t```\n137\t\n138\t## The Correctness Oracle\n139\t\n140\tgribtract is built **oracle-first**: correctness is defined as agreement with eccodes/wgrib2, not as passing hand-written unit tests.\n141\t\n142\tThe differential harness in `gribtract-testutil` runs both decoders over a corpus of real NOAA GRIB2 files (GFS, HRRR, NBM, GEFS), then compares:\n143\t\n144\t- **Metadata exactly** — center, parameter ID, level, forecast time, grid geometry\n145\t- **Grid values within derived tolerance** — tolerance is computed from the packing template's own scale factors, not an arbitrary epsilon\n146\t\n147\tAny message where gribtract disagrees with the reference decoder becomes a permanent regression fixture. The agreement percentage can only increase; there is no mechanism to silently remove a covered template.\n148\t\n149\t```\n150\tgribtract decode  ─┐\n151\t                   ├─→ compare field-by-field + grid within tolerance\n152\teccodes/wgrib2    ─┘\n153\t```\n154\t\n155\tRun the harness:\n156\t\n157\t```bash\n158\tcargo test -p gribtract -- differential\n159\t```\n160\t\n161\tFetch and run against the full corpus (requires eccodes installed):\n162\t\n163\t```bash\n164\tcargo xtask corpus fetch\n165\tcargo xtask corpus verify\n166\t```\n167\t\n168\t## Performance Dashboard\n169\t\n170\tA self-contained benchmark dashboard (`dashboard.html`) tracks decode throughput on real NOAA files, fed by `bench-results.json`. Speed numbers are never reported without a corresponding correctness verification run.\n171\t\n172\t```bash\n173\t# Run benchmarks and regenerate bench-results.json\n174\tcargo run --bin xtask -- bench\n175\t\n176\t# Serve the live dashboard with streaming benchmark updates\n177\tcargo run --bin xtask -- serve\n178\t```\n179\t\n180\t## Building\n181\t\n182\tRequires Rust 1.75+.\n183\t\n184\t```bash\n185\tgit clone https://github.com/jedarden/gribtract\n186\tcd gribtract\n187\tcargo build --release\n188\t```\n189\t\n190\tThe `gribtract-py` Python extension is excluded from the default workspace build (it requires Python headers for PyO3). Build it explicitly:\n191\t\n192\t```bash\n193\tpip install maturin\n194\tmaturin develop -p gribtract-py\n195\t```\n196\t\n197\t## CLI\n198\t\n199\t```bash\n200\t# List all fields in a GRIB2 file (JSON output)\n201\tgribtract list forecast.grib2\n202\t\n203\t# Decode all fields and print as JSON\n204\tgribtract decode forecast.grib2\n205\t\n206\t# Dump grid values for a specific field index\n207\tgribtract dump forecast.grib2 --field 0 --format csv\n208\t```\n209\t\n210\tThe CLI binary is at `crates/gribtract-cli`. It is a thin wrapper over the library API and is intended for ad-hoc inspection rather than production use.\n211\t\n212\t## License\n213\t\n214\tMIT OR Apache-2.0\n215\t"}]},"parent_tool_use_id":null,"session_id":"319cc968-0185-491a-b21f-0a58c94f2ab7","uuid":"7fe5dfe1-2b60-423b-9c2d-235ab8b79552","timestamp":"2026-07-02T15:06:54.357Z","tool_use_result":{"type":"text","file":{"filePath":"/home/coding/gribtract/README.md","content":"# gribtract\n\nA pure-Rust GRIB2 decoder — decodes NOAA/WMO GRIB2 messages into typed fields and gridded numeric data, verified field-by-field against eccodes/wgrib2.\n\nNo C dependencies for core GRIB2 decoding (DRT 5.0, 5.2, 5.3, 5.41). No shelling out to `wgrib2`. JPEG2000 (DRT 5.40) requires the optional `jpeg2000` feature, which pulls in `openjpeg-sys` (C FFI).\n\n## What is GRIB2?\n\nGRIB2 (GRIdded Binary, Edition 2) is the binary file format used by NOAA, ECMWF, and other national weather services to distribute model output — temperature, wind, precipitation, pressure fields on global and regional grids. It is the primary output format for GFS, HRRR, NBM, GEFS, NAM, and similar NWP (numerical weather prediction) products.\n\nDecoding GRIB2 requires handling: multi-section message framing, grid geometry definitions (lat/lon, Lambert conformal, polar stereographic, Gaussian), parameter tables indexed by discipline/category/number, five or more data packing algorithms (simple, complex, spatial-differencing, JPEG2000, PNG), and bitmaps for missing-value masking.\n\nThe standard tools (`wgrib2`, `eccodes`) are C programs and libraries. If you need to decode GRIB2 in a Rust project, your options have been to shell out to them, call their C APIs through FFI, or skip decoded values and work with raw bytes.\n\ngribtract is a native Rust alternative.\n\n## Features\n\n- **Pure Rust for core formats** — no C toolchain or FFI for DRT 5.0, 5.2, 5.3, 5.41 (simple, complex, complex+spatial differencing, PNG). JPEG2000 (DRT 5.40) requires the optional `jpeg2000` feature, which uses `openjpeg-sys` (C FFI)\n- **Typed output** — decoded fields carry structured metadata: parameter ID, level, forecast time, grid geometry\n- **Multiple packing formats** — DRT 5.0 (simple), 5.2 (complex), 5.3 (complex+spatial differencing), 5.40 (JPEG2000), 5.41 (PNG)\n- **Multiple grid projections** — lat/lon (GDT 3.0), polar stereographic (3.20), Lambert conformal (3.30), Gaussian (3.40)\n- **Lazy decode path** — parse headers and defer grid data; extract individual grid points in O(1) for DRT=0 fields without full decode\n- **Timeseries extractor** — given a decoded GRIB2 message set, extract per-station forecasts for a list of lat/lon coordinates\n- **Python bindings** — `gribtract-py` (PyO3) exposes the Rust decoder to Python\n- **Oracle-gated correctness** — every release is verified against eccodes/wgrib2 over a corpus of real NOAA files\n\n## Crate Layout\n\n```\ngribtract/\n├── crates/\n│   ├── gribtract-core     # Section parser, template decoders, unpacking. No I/O.\n│   ├── gribtract          # High-level API: message iterator, field selection, timeseries\n│   ├── gribtract-cli      # `gribtract decode|list|dump` command-line tool\n│   ├── gribtract-py       # Python bindings via PyO3 (excluded from default build)\n│   └── gribtract-testutil # Differential harness utilities: corpus, golden, diff\n└── xtask                  # Corpus management, reference-decoder runner, bench dashboard\n```\n\n`gribtract-core` is the zero-dependency parsing core. `gribtract` is the crate you depend on for library use. `gribtract-cli` provides a standalone binary.\n\n## Usage\n\nAdd to `Cargo.toml`:\n\n```toml\n[dependencies]\ngribtract = \"0.1\"\n```\n\n### Decode all fields from a GRIB2 file\n\n```rust\nuse gribtract::{decode, Field};\n\nlet bytes = std::fs::read(\"hrrr.t00z.wrfsfcf01.grib2\")?;\nlet fields: Vec<Field> = gribtract::decode(&bytes)?;\n\nfor field in &fields {\n    println!(\n        \"param={}/{}/{}  level={}  forecast_offset={}h\",\n        field.parameter.discipline,\n        field.parameter.category,\n        field.parameter.number,\n        field.level.value1(),\n        field.forecast_time.offset_seconds() / 3600,\n    );\n}\n```\n\n### Extract a single grid point (DRT=0, O(1))\n\n```rust\nuse gribtract::{decode_lazy, decode_point_drt0};\n\nlet fields = gribtract::decode_lazy(&bytes)?;\nfor lf in &fields {\n    if let Some(body) = &lf.section7_raw {\n        // idx is the flat grid index for your target lat/lon\n        let idx = lf.grid.nearest_index(40.64, -73.78)?;\n        if let Some(value) = decode_point_drt0(body, &lf.packing, idx) {\n            println!(\"value at JFK: {}\", value);\n        }\n    }\n}\n```\n\n### Extract a forecast timeseries for a list of stations\n\n```rust\nuse gribtract::timeseries::{extract_timeseries, ParameterRecord, Station, TimeseriesRequest};\n\n// 2m temperature (discipline=0, category=0, number=0), height-above-ground level (type=103)\nlet request = TimeseriesRequest {\n    parameter: ParameterRecord { discipline: 0, category: 0, number: 0 },\n    level_type1: 103,\n    stations: vec![\n        Station { id: \"JFK\".into(),  lat: 40.64,  lon: -73.78  },\n        Station { id: \"ORD\".into(),  lat: 41.979, lon: -87.905 },\n        Station { id: \"LAX\".into(),  lat: 33.943, lon: -118.408 },\n    ],\n};\n\nlet fields = gribtract::decode(&bytes)?;\nlet ts = extract_timeseries(&fields, &request);\n\nfor (station, rows) in &ts.stations {\n    println!(\"Station: {}\", station);\n    for row in rows {\n        println!(\"  forecast hour {}: {:?}\", row.forecast_hour, row.value);\n    }\n}\n```\n\n### Decode a DRT=3 (complex+spatial differencing) grid — extract many stations\n\nDRT=3 requires a full sequential decode; random access is not possible. The recommended pattern is decode-once-extract-many:\n\n```rust\nuse gribtract::{decode_lazy, decode_all_drt3};\n\nlet lazy_fields = gribtract::decode_lazy(&bytes)?;\nfor lf in &lazy_fields {\n    if let (Some(body), Some(extra)) = (&lf.section7_raw, &lf.complex_extra) {\n        let n = lf.grid.num_data_points as usize;\n        let grid: Vec<f64> = decode_all_drt3(body, &lf.packing, extra, n)?;\n\n        // Now extract any number of stations by index in O(1) each\n        for (lat, lon) in &station_coords {\n            let idx = lf.grid.nearest_index(*lat, *lon)?;\n            println!(\"value: {}\", grid[idx]);\n        }\n    }\n}\n```\n\n## The Correctness Oracle\n\ngribtract is built **oracle-first**: correctness is defined as agreement with eccodes/wgrib2, not as passing hand-written unit tests.\n\nThe differential harness in `gribtract-testutil` runs both decoders over a corpus of real NOAA GRIB2 files (GFS, HRRR, NBM, GEFS), then compares:\n\n- **Metadata exactly** — center, parameter ID, level, forecast time, grid geometry\n- **Grid values within derived tolerance** — tolerance is computed from the packing template's own scale factors, not an arbitrary epsilon\n\nAny message where gribtract disagrees with the reference decoder becomes a permanent regression fixture. The agreement percentage can only increase; there is no mechanism to silently remove a covered template.\n\n```\ngribtract decode  ─┐\n                   ├─→ compare field-by-field + grid within tolerance\neccodes/wgrib2    ─┘\n```\n\nRun the harness:\n\n```bash\ncargo test -p gribtract -- differential\n```\n\nFetch and run against the full corpus (requires eccodes installed):\n\n```bash\ncargo xtask corpus fetch\ncargo xtask corpus verify\n```\n\n## Performance Dashboard\n\nA self-contained benchmark dashboard (`dashboard.html`) tracks decode throughput on real NOAA files, fed by `bench-results.json`. Speed numbers are never reported without a corresponding correctness verification run.\n\n```bash\n# Run benchmarks and regenerate bench-results.json\ncargo run --bin xtask -- bench\n\n# Serve the live dashboard with streaming benchmark updates\ncargo run --bin xtask -- serve\n```\n\n## Building\n\nRequires Rust 1.75+.\n\n```bash\ngit clone https://github.com/jedarden/gribtract\ncd gribtract\ncargo build --release\n```\n\nThe `gribtract-py` Python extension is excluded from the default workspace build (it requires Python headers for PyO3). Build it explicitly:\n\n```bash\npip install maturin\nmaturin develop -p gribtract-py\n```\n\n## CLI\n\n```bash\n# List all fields in a GRIB2 file (JSON output)\ngribtract list forecast.grib2\n\n# Decode all fields and print as JSON\ngribtract decode forecast.grib2\n\n# Dump grid values for a specific field index\ngribtract dump forecast.grib2 --field 0 --format csv\n```\n\nThe CLI binary is at `crates/gribtract-cli`. It is a thin wrapper over the library API and is intended for ad-hoc inspection rather than production use.\n\n## License\n\nMIT OR Apache-2.0\n","numLines":215,"startLine":1,"totalLines":215}}}

 data = f.read()\n10\t//!\n11\t//! fields = gribtract.decode(data)\n12\t//! for field in fields:\n13\t//!     print(field.discipline, field.category, field.number,\n14\t//!           \"->\", }\n102\t\n103\t    /// Physical value of fixed surface 1.\n104\t    #[getter]\n105\t    fn level_value1(&self) -> f64 {\n106\t        self.inner.level.value1()\n107\t    }\n108\t\n109\t    /// Fixed of fixed surface 2.\n116\t    #[getter]\n117\t    fn level_value2(&self) -> f64 {\n118\t        self.inner.level.value2()\n119\t    }\n120\t\n121\t    // ── Ensemble deterministic products.\n125\t    #[getter]\n126\t    fn ensemble_member_type(&self) -> Option<u8> {\n127\t        self.inner.ensemble.map(|e| e.member_type)\n128\t    }\n129\t\n130\t    /// Ensemble #[getter]\n140\t    fn gdt_template(&self) -> u16 {\n141\t        self.inner.gdt_template\n142\t    }\n143\t\n144\t    /// Product Definition Template number (Table 4.0).\n145\t    #[getter]\n146\t Python bindings for gribtract — pure-Rust GRIB2 decoder.\n//!\n//! # Usage\n//!\n//! ```python\n//! import gribtract\n//!\n//! with open(\"sample.grib2\", \"rb\") as f:\n//!     data = points\")\n//! ```\n\nuse pyo3::exceptions::PyValueError;\nuse pyo3::prelude::*;\n\nuse gribtract_core::types::{Field, GridValues};\nuse ::gribtract::decode as gribtract_decode;\n\n/// GRIB2 decoded field exposed to Python.\n///\n/// Attributes correspond to the WMO GRIB2 table numbers cited in the field\n/// docs; see the gribtract-core source for definitions.\n#[pyclass(name = \"Field\")]\nstruct PyField {\n    inner: Field,\n}\n\n#[pymethods]\nimpl PyField {\n    // ── Identification ──────────────────────────────────────────────────\n\n    /// Originating centre (Table 0.0; 7 = NCEP/NWS).\n    #[getter]\n    fn center(&self) -> u16 {\n        self.inner.center\n    }\n\n    /// Originating sub-centre.\n    #[getter]\n    fn subcenter(&self) -> u16 {\n        self.inner.subcenter\n    }\n\n    // ── Parameter ───────────────────────────────────────────────────────\n\n    /// Discipline (Table 0.0): 0 = meteorological, 1 = hydrological, 10 = oceanographic.\n    #[getter]\n    fn discipline(&self) -> u8 {\n        self.inner.parameter.discipline\n    }\n\n    /// Parameter category within the discipline (Table 4.1.x).\n    #[getter]\n    fn category(&self) -> u8 {\n        self.inner.parameter.category\n    }\n\n    /// Parameter number within the category (Table 4.2.x.y).\n    #[getter]\n    fn number(&self) -> u8 {\n        self.inner.parameter.number\n    }\n\n    // ── Reference time ──────────────────────────────────────────────────\n\n    /// Reference time as a `(year, month, day, hour, minute, second)` tuple.\n    #[getter]\n    fn ref_time(&self) -> (u16, u8, u8, u8, u8, u8) {\n        let rt = &self.inner.forecast.reference_time;\n        (rt.year, rt.month, rt.day, rt.hour, rt.minute, rt.second)\n    }\n\n    /// Unix timestamp of the reference time (seconds since 1970-01-01T00:00:00Z).\n    #[getter]\n    fn ref_time_unix(&self) -> i64 {\n        self.inner.forecast.reference_time.unix_seconds()\n    }\n\n    /// Forecast offset in seconds.\n    #[getter]\n    fn forecast_offset_seconds(&self) -> i64 {\n        self.inner.forecast.offset_seconds()\n    }\n\n    /// Unix timestamp of the valid (verifying) time.\n    #[getter]\n    fn valid_time_unix(&self) -> i64 {\n        self.inner.forecast.valid_unix_seconds()\n    }\n\n    // ── Level ───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────\n\n    /// Fixed surface 1 type code (Table 4.5; 100 = isobaric, 103 = height above MSL, …).\n    #[getter]\n    fn level_type1(&self) -> u8 surface 2 type code (255 = not applicable / missing).\n    #[getter]\n    fn level_type2(&self) -> u8 {\n        self.inner.level.type2\n    }\n\n    /// Physical value of fixed surface 2.\n    #[getter]\n    fn level_value2(&self) -> f64 {\n        self.inner.level.value2()\n    }\n\n    // ── Ensemble ────────────────────────────────────────────────────────\n\n    /// Ensemble member type (Table 4.6): 0 = unperturbed, 1 = pos-perturbed, 2 = neg-perturbed.\n    /// `None` for deterministic products.\n    #[getter]\n    fn ensemble_member_type(&self) -> Option<u8> {\n        self.inner.ensemble.map(|e| e.member_type)\n    }\n\n    /// Ensemble member number. `None` for deterministic products.\n    #[getter]\n    fn ensemble_member(&self) -> Option<i16> {\n        self.inner.ensemble.map(|e| e.number)\n    }\n\n    // ── Grid geometry ────────────────────────────────────────────────────\n\n    /// Grid Definition Template number (Table 3.1): 0=lat/lon, 20=polar pdt_template(&self) -> u16 {\n        self.inner.pdt_template\n    }\n\n    /// Data Representation Template number (Table 5.0):\n    /// 0=simple, 2=complex, 3=spatial-diff, 40=JPEG2000, 41=PNG.\n    #[getter]\n    fn drt_template(&self) -> u16 {\n        self.inner.drt_template\n    }\n\n    /// Total number of grid data points (including masked / missing points).\n    #[getter]\n    fn #[getter]\n    fn lon_first(&self) -> f64 {\n        self.inner.grid.lon_first\n    }\n\n    /// Latitude of the last grid point.\n    #[getter]\n    fn lat_last(&self) -> f64 {\n        self.inner.grid.lat_last\n    }\n\n    /// Longitude of the last grid point.\n    #[getter]\n    fn lon_last(&self) -> f64 {\n        self.inner.grid.lon_last\n    }\n\n    /// i-direction (longitude) grids.\n    #[getter]\n    fn dj(&self) -> f64 {\n        self.inner.grid.dj\n    }\n\n    // ── Decoded values ───────────────────────────────────────────────────\n\n    /// Decoded grid values as a Python list of floats.\n    ///\n    /// For masked grids (`GridValues::Masked`), returns only the *present*\n    /// values in scan order. Use `values_with_mask()` to obtain separate\n    /// value and mask arrays.\n    #[getter]\n    fn values(&self) -> Vec<f64> {\n        match &self.inner.values {\n            GridValues::Dense(v) => v.clone(),\n            GridValues::Masked { values, present } => {\n                // Return only present values\n                values\n                    .iter()\n                    .zip(present.iter())\n                    .filter_map(|(v, p)| if *p { Some(*v) } else { None })\n                    .collect()\n            }\n        }\n    }\n\n    /// Returns `(values, mask)` where both lists have length `num_data_points`.\n    ///\n    /// For dense grids the mask is all-True. For masked grids, a `False` entry\n    /// means the corresponding point is missing / not decoded.\n    fn values_with_mask(&self) -> (Vec<f64>, Vec<bool>) {\n        match &self.inner.values {\n            GridValues::Dense(v) => {\n                let mask = vec![true; v.len()];\n                (v.clone(), mask)\n            }\n            GridValues::Masked { values, present } => {\n missing values).\n    #[getter]\n    fn is_dense(&self) -> bool {\n        matches!(&self.inner.values, GridValues::Dense(_))\n    }\n\n    fn __repr__(&self) -> String {\n        let rt = self.ref_time();\n        let n = match &self.inner.values {\n            GridValues::Dense(v) => v.len(),\n            GridValues::Masked { values, .. } => values.len(),\n        };\n        format!(\n            \"Field(discipline={}, category={}, number={}, \\\n             level_type1={}, level_value1={:.1}, \\\n             valid_time={:04}-{:02}-{:02}T{:02}:{:02}Z, \\\n             drt={}, nx={}, ny={}, n_values={})\",\n            self.discipline(),\n            self.category(),\n            self.number(),\n            self.level_type1(),\n            self.level_value1(),\n            rt.0, rt.1, rt.2, rt.3, rt.4,\n            self.drt_template(),\n            self.nx(),\n            self.ny(),\n            n,\n        )\n    }\n}\n\n// ── Module ───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────\n\n/// Decode all GRIB2 fields from raw bytes.\n///\n/// Parameters\n/// ----------\n/// data : bytes\n///     Raw GRIB2 bytes (may contain multiple concatenated messages).\n///\n/// Returns\n/// -------\n/// list[Field]\n///     Decoded fields in message order.\n///\n/// Raises\n/// ------\n/// ValueError\n///     If the bytes are not valid GRIB2.\n#[pyfunction]\nfn decode(data: &[u8]) -> PyResult<Vec<PyField>> {\n    gribtract_decode(data)\n        .map(|fields| fields.into_iter().map(|f| PyField { inner: f }).collect())\n        .map_err(|e| PyValueError::new_err(e.to_string()))\n}\n\n/// gribtract — pure-Rust GRIB2 decoder Python bindings.\n///\n/// Quick start\n/// -----------\n/// ```python\n/// import \"gribtract\")]\nfn gribtract_module(m: &Bound<'_, PyModule>) -> PyResult<()> {\n    m.add_class::<PyField>()?;\n    m.add_function(wrap_pyfunction!(decode, m)?)?;\n    Ok(())\n}\n\n// ── Rust-side workspace root\n            .unwrap()\n            .to_path_buf();\n        root.join(\"tests/corpus/small\").join(name)\n    }\n\n    #[test]\n    fn decode_drt0_returns_fields() {\n        let path Python bindings for gribtract — pure-Rust GRIB2 decoder.\n//!\n//! # Usage\n//!\n//! ```python\n//! import gribtract\n//!\n//! with open(\"sample.grib2\", \"rb\") as f:\n//!     data = f.read()\n//!\n//! fields = gribtract.decode(data)\n//! for field in fields:\n//!     print(field.discipline, field.category, field.number,\n//!           \"->\", len(field.values), \"grid points\")\n//! ```\n\nuse pyo3::exceptions::PyValueError;\nuse pyo3::prelude::*;\n\nuse gribtract_core::types::{Field, GridValues};\nuse ::gribtract::decode as gribtract_decode;\n\n/// GRIB2 decoded field exposed to Python.\n///\n/// Attributes correspond to the WMO GRIB2 table numbers cited in the field\n/// docs; see the gribtract-core source for definitions.\n#[pyclass(name = \"Field\")]\nstruct PyField {\n    inner: Field,\n}\n\n#[pymethods]\nimpl PyField {\n    // ── Identification ──────────────────────────────────────────────────\n\n    /// Originating centre (Table 0.0; 7 = NCEP/NWS).\n    #[getter]\n    fn center(&self) -> u16 {\n        self.inner.center\n    }\n\n    /// Originating sub-centre.\n    #[getter]\n    fn subcenter(&self) -> u16 {\n        self.inner.subcenter\n    }\n\n    // ── Parameter ───────────────────────────────────────────────────────\n\n    /// Discipline (Table 0.0): 0 = meteorological, 1 = hydrological, 10 = oceanographic.\n    #[getter]\n    fn discipline(&self) -> u8 {\n        self.inner.parameter.discipline\n    }\n\n    /// Parameter category within the discipline (Table 4.1.x).\n    #[getter]\n    fn category(&self) -> u8 {\n        self.inner.parameter.category\n    }\n\n    /// Parameter number within the category (Table 4.2.x.y).\n    #[getter]\n    fn number(&self) -> u8 {\n        self.inner.parameter.number\n    }\n\n    // ── Reference time ──────────────────────────────────────────────────\n\n    /// Reference time as a `(year, month, day, hour, minute, second)` tuple.\n    #[getter]\n    fn ref_time(&self) -> (u16, u8, u8, u8, u8, u8) {\n        let rt = &self.inner.forecast.reference_time;\n        (rt.year, rt.month, rt.day, rt.hour, rt.minute, rt.second)\n    }\n\n    /// Unix timestamp of the reference time (seconds since 1970-01-01T00:00:00Z).\n    #[getter]\n    fn ref_time_unix(&self) -> i64 {\n        self.inner.forecast.reference_time.unix_seconds()\n    }\n\n    /// Forecast offset in seconds.\n    #[getter]\n    fn forecast_offset_seconds(&self) -> i64 {\n        self.inner.forecast.offset_seconds()\n    }\n\n    /// Unix timestamp of the valid (verifying) time.\n    #[getter]\n    fn valid_time_unix(&self) -> i64 {\n        self.inner.forecast.valid_unix_seconds()\n    }\n\n    // ── Level ───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────\n\n    /// Fixed surface 1 type code (Table 4.5; 100 = isobaric, 103 = height above MSL, …).\n    #[getter]\n    fn level_type1(&self) -> u8 {\n        self.inner.level.type1\n    }\n\n    /// Physical value of fixed surface 1.\n    #[getter]\n    fn level_value1(&self) -> f64 {\n        self.inner.level.value1()\n    }\n\n    /// Fixed surface 2 type code (255 = not applicable / missing).\n    #[getter]\n    fn level_type2(&self) -> u8 {\n        self.inner.level.type2\n    }\n\n    /// Physical value of fixed surface 2.\n    #[getter]\n    fn level_value2(&self) -> f64 {\n        self.inner.level.value2()\n    }\n\n    // ── Ensemble ────────────────────────────────────────────────────────\n\n    /// Ensemble member type (Table 4.6): 0 = unperturbed, 1 = pos-perturbed, 2 = neg-perturbed.\n    /// `None` for deterministic products.\n    #[getter]\n    fn ensemble_member_type(&self) -> Option<u8> {\n        self.inner.ensemble.map(|e| e.member_type)\n    }\n\n    /// Ensemble member number. `None` for deterministic products.\n    #[getter]\n    fn ensemble_member(&self) -> Option<i16> {\n        self.inner.ensemble.map(|e| e.number)\n    }\n\n    // ── Grid geometry ────────────────────────────────────────────────────\n\n    /// Grid Definition Template number (Table 3.1): 0=lat/lon, 20=polar stereo, 30=Lambert.\n    #[getter]\n    fn gdt_template(&self) -> u16 {\n        self.inner.gdt_template\n    }\n\n    /// Product Definition Template number (Table 4.0).\n    #[getter]\n    fn pdt_template(&self) -> u16 {\n        self.inner.pdt_template\n    }\n\n    /// Data Representation Template number (Table 5.0):\n    /// 0=simple, 2=complex, 3=spatial-diff, 40=JPEG2000, 41=PNG.\n    #[getter]\n    fn drt_template(&self) -> u16 {\n        self.inner.drt_template\n    }\n\n    /// Total number of grid data points (including masked / missing points).\n    #[getter]\n    fn num_data_points(&self) -> u32 {\n        self.inner.grid.num_data_points\n    }\n\n    /// Number of columns (i-direction points).\n    #[getter]\n    fn nx(&self) -> u32 {\n        self.inner.grid.nx\n    }\n\n    /// Number of rows (j-direction points).\n    #[getter]\n    fn ny(&self) -> u32 {\n        self.inner.grid.ny\n    }\n\n    /// Latitude of the first grid point (degrees, positive North).\n    #[getter]\n    fn lat_first(&self) -> f64 {\n        self.inner.grid.lat_first\n    }\n\n    /// Longitude of the first grid point (degrees, positive East, 0–360).\n    #[getter]\n    fn lon_first(&self) -> f64 {\n        self.inner.grid.lon_first\n    }\n\n    /// Latitude of the last grid point.\n    #[getter]\n    fn lat_last(&self) -> f64 {\n        self.inner.grid.lat_last\n    }\n\n    /// Longitude of the last grid point.\n    #[getter]\n    fn lon_last(&self) -> f64 {\n        self.inner.grid.lon_last\n    }\n\n    /// i-direction (longitude) grid spacing in degrees. 0 for projected grids.\n    #[getter]\n    fn di(&self) -> f64 {\n        self.inner.grid.di\n    }\n\n    /// j-direction (latitude) grid spacing in degrees. 0 for projected grids.\n    #[getter]\n    fn dj(&self) -> f64 {\n        self.inner.grid.dj\n    }\n\n    // ── Decoded values ───────────────────────────────────────────────────\n\n    /// Decoded grid values as a Python list of floats.\n    ///\n    /// For masked grids (`GridValues::Masked`), returns only the *present*\n    /// values in scan order. Use `values_with_mask()` to obtain separate\n    /// value and mask arrays.\n    #[getter]\n    fn values(&self) -> Vec<f64> {\n        match &self.inner.values {\n            GridValues::Dense(v) => v.clone(),\n            GridValues::Masked { values, present } => {\n                // Return only present values\n                values\n                    .iter()\n                    .zip(present.iter())\n                    .filter_map(|(v, p)| if *p { Some(*v) } else { None })\n                    .collect()\n            }\n        }\n    }\n\n    /// Returns `(values, mask)` where both lists have length `num_data_points`.\n    ///\n    /// For dense grids the mask is all-True. For masked grids, a `False` entry\n    /// means the corresponding point is missing / not decoded.\n    fn values_with_mask(&self) -> (Vec<f64>, Vec<bool>) {\n        match &self.inner.values {\n            GridValues::Dense(v) => {\n                let mask = vec![true; v.len()];\n                (v.clone(), mask)\n            }\n            GridValues::Masked { values, present } => {\n                let mask: Vec<bool> = present.iter().copied().collect();\n                (values.clone(), mask)\n            }\n        }\n    }\n\n    /// True when all grid points are present (no bitmap / missing values).\n    #[getter]\n    fn is_dense(&self) -> bool {\n        matches!(&self.inner.values, GridValues::Dense(_))\n    }\n\n    fn __repr__(&self) -> String {\n        let rt = self.ref_time();\n        let n = match &self.inner.values {\n            GridValues::Dense(v) => v.len(),\n            GridValues::Masked { values, .. } => values.len(),\n        };\n        format!(\n            \"Field(discipline={}, category={}, number={}, \\\n             level_type1={}, level_value1={:.1}, \\\n             valid_time={:04}-{:02}-{:02}T{:02}:{:02}Z, \\\n             drt={}, nx={}, ny={}, n_values={})\",\n            self.discipline(),\n            self.category(),\n            self.number(),\n            self.level_type1(),\n            self.level_value1(),\n            rt.0, rt.1, rt.2, rt.3, rt.4,\n            self.drt_template(),\n            self.nx(),\n            self.ny(),\n            n,\n        )\n    }\n}\n\n// ── Module ───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────\n\n/// Decode all GRIB2 fields from raw bytes.\n///\n/// Parameters\n/// ----------\n/// data : bytes\n///     Raw GRIB2 bytes (may contain multiple concatenated messages).\n///\n/// Returns\n/// -------\n/// list[Field]\n///     Decoded fields in message order.\n///\n/// Raises\n/// ------\n/// ValueError\n///     If the bytes are not valid GRIB2.\n#[pyfunction]\nfn decode(data: &[u8]) -> PyResult<Vec<PyField>> {\n    gribtract_decode(data)\n        .map(|fields| fields.into_iter().map(|f| PyField { inner: f }).collect())\n        .map_err(|e| PyValueError::new_err(e.to_string()))\n}\n\n/// gribtract — pure-Rust GRIB2 decoder Python bindings.\n///\n/// Quick start\n/// -----------\n/// ```python\n/// import gribtract\n///\n/// with open(\"sample.grib2\", \"rb\") as f:\n///     fields = gribtract.decode(f.read())\n///\n/// for f in fields:\n///     print(f)\n/// ```\n#[pymodule]\n#[pyo3(name = \"gribtract\")]\nfn gribtract_module(m: &Bound<'_, PyModule>) -> PyResult<()> {\n    m.add_class::<PyField>()?;\n    m.add_function(wrap_pyfunction!(decode, m)?)?;\n    Ok(())\n}\n\n// ── Rust-side unit tests ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────\n// These tests run via `cargo test` and verify that the Rust decode path\n// called by the Python bindings produces correct results on the small\n// test fixtures shipped in the workspace corpus.\n\n#[cfg(test)]\nmod tests {\n    use super::*;\n\n    fn corpus_path(name: &str) -> std::path::PathBuf {\n        // Fixture lives at <workspace_root>/tests/corpus/small/<name>\n        let root = std::path::Path::new(env!(\"CARGO_MANIFEST_DIR\"))\n            .parent()  // crates/\n            .unwrap()\n            .parent()  // workspace root\n            .unwrap()\n            .to_path_buf();\n        root.join(\"tests/corpus/small\").join(name)\n    }\n\n    #[test]\n    fn decode_drt0_returns_fields() {\n        let path = corpus_path(\"gfs_anl_t2m_5x5.grib2\");\n        let data = std::fs::read(&path)\n            .unwrap_or_else(|_| panic!(\"missing fixture: {}\", path.display()));\n        let fields = gribtract_decode(&data).expect(\"decode failed\");\n        assert!(!fields.is_empty(), \"expected at least one field\");\n        let f = &fields[0];\n        // DRT=0 simple packing — values should be dense\n        assert!(matches!(f.values, GridValues::Dense(_)),\n            \"expected Dense values for DRT=0 fixture\");\n        // Grid should have points\n        assert!(f.grid.num_data_points > 0);\n    }\n\n    #[test]\n    fn decode_drt40_jpeg2000_returns_fields() {\n        let path = corpus_path(\"drt40_j2k_3x2.grib2\");\n        let data = std::fs::read(&path)\n            .unwrap_or_else(|_| panic!(\"missing fixture: {}\", path.display()));\n        let fields = gribtract_decode(&data).expect(\"decode failed\");\n        assert!(!fields.is_empty(), \"expected at least one field from DRT=40 fixture\");\n        assert_eq!(fields[0].drt_template, 40, \"expected DRT=40\");\n    }\n\n    #[test]\n    fn decode_drt41_png_returns_fields() {\n        let path = corpus_path(\"drt41_png_3x2.grib2\");\n        let data = std::fs::read(&path)\n            .unwrap_or_else(|_| panic!(\"missing fixture: {}\", path.display()));\n        let fields = gribtract_decode(&data).expect(\"decode failed\");\n        assert!(!fields.is_empty(), \"expected at least one field from DRT=41 fixture\");\n        assert_eq!(fields[0].drt_template, 41, \"expected DRT=41\");\n    }\n\n    #[test]\n    fn pyfiled_repr_does_not_panic() {\n        // Smoke test: build a PyField from a decoded field and call __repr__\n        let path = corpus_path(\"gfs_anl_t2m_5x5.grib2\");\n        let data = match std::fs::read(&path) {\n            Ok(d) => d,\n            Err(_) => return, // skip if fixture missing\n        };\n        let fields = gribtract_decode(&data).expect(\"decode failed\");\n        let py_field = PyField { inner: fields.into_iter().next().unwrap() };\n        let repr =
